hints: A butterfly has the same price whether built from calls or puts (put–call parity makes the linear terms cancel).
Compute the call butterfly: C(10) − 2C(15) + C(20).
solution: A $10/15/20$ butterfly costs the same with puts or calls (the stock and bond terms from put–call parity cancel in the $1{:}{-}2{:}1$ combination). Using calls: $C_{10}-2C_{15}+C_{20}=8.1-8.4+1.4=1.1.$

hints: Per draw, A earns (100−a)·a/(a+b). Find the symmetric Nash equilibrium a=b.
solution: Per draw A's expected payout is $A(a,b)=\dfrac{a}{a+b}(100-a)$. Setting $\partial_a A=0$ gives $a^2+2ab-100b=0$; at the symmetric equilibrium $a=b$ this is $3a^2=100a\Rightarrow a=\tfrac{100}{3}$. Each draw then pays $\tfrac12(100-\tfrac{100}3)=\tfrac{100}{3}$, and with two draws A's total is $2\cdot\tfrac{100}{3}=\tfrac{200}{3}\approx67.$

hints: N≥k means the first k−1 values are non-increasing.
P(X_1≥…≥X_{k-1})=1/(k−1)! (all orderings equally likely).
solution: $N\ge k$ exactly when $X_1\ge X_2\ge\dots\ge X_{k-1}$, which for continuous IID values has probability $\tfrac1{(k-1)!}$. Then $\mathbb{E}[N]=\sum_{k\ge1}P(N\ge k)=\sum_{k\ge1}\tfrac1{(k-1)!}=e$, so $\ln\mathbb{E}[N]=1.$

hints: Once you see HH, the next T completes HHT (friend) before any HTH is possible.
Track states; let q=P(friend wins) from state 'just saw H'.
solution: Friend wins if HHT appears before HTH. From the state 'last flip H': a further H reaches HH, from which the next T gives HHT before any HTH can form (friend wins for sure); a T reaches HT, from which H gives HTH (you win) and T returns to needing an H. Letting $q$ be the friend's win probability from 'saw H': $q=\tfrac12\cdot1+\tfrac12\big(\tfrac12\cdot0+\tfrac12 q\big)$, so $\tfrac34 q=\tfrac12\Rightarrow q=\tfrac23.$

hints: Net progress is +2 ft/hour, but the snail eats the instant it reaches 30 (before the wind).
After 13 hours it's at 26 ft; in hour 14 it climbs +4 to reach 30.
14 hours.
solution: Each hour the snail gains $4$ then loses $2$ (net $+2$), but reaches the food the instant it touches $30$ — before the gust. After $13$ hours it sits at $2\cdot13=26$ ft; in the $14$th hour it climbs $+4$ to $30$ and eats. So it takes $14$ hours.

hints: Let t be the time before the 1PM meeting; after meeting the ships take 4h and 9h to finish.
A standard relation gives t=√(t₁·t₂)=√(4·9).
t=6h before 1PM ⇒ 7:00 AM ⇒ 700.
solution: If $t$ is the travel time to the meeting, the post-meeting times satisfy $t=\sqrt{t_1 t_2}=\sqrt{4\cdot9}=6$ hours (each ship covers the other's pre-meeting distance). So they left $6$ hours before $1{:}00$ PM, i.e. $7{:}00$ AM $=700.$

hints: On the circle, point 0 is reached by hitting either 0 or 100 on the unrolled line (100≡0).
Starting at 1 with absorbing boundaries 0 and 100, expected time = position·(100−position).
solution: Unroll the circle to a line with absorbing points $0$ and $100$ (which are the same point). Starting at $1$, the expected time for a symmetric walk to hit $\{0,100\}$ is $1\cdot(100-1)=99.$

hints: Fix the first flip as heads; it leads iff there are >5 heads total, i.e. ≥5 heads in the remaining 9.
For Binom(9,½), P(X≥5)=P(X≤4)=1/2 by symmetry.
So the probability is 1/2.
solution: Say the first flip is heads. It is 'leading' after $10$ flips iff heads strictly exceed $5$, i.e. at least $5$ heads occur in the remaining $9$ flips. For $\mathrm{Binom}(9,\tfrac12)$, $P(X\ge5)=P(X\le4)=\tfrac12$ by symmetry, so the answer is $\tfrac12.$

hints: The n-th line crosses the previous n−1, adding n new regions.
solution: Each new line meets all previous ones, adding as many regions as it has pieces. This gives $L_n=1+n+\binom n2=1+\tfrac{n(n+1)}2$. For $n=10$: $1+55=56.$

hints: Digits are prime {2,3,5,7}; product 100000=2⁵·5⁵ has no factor 3 or 7.
So digits must be only 2s and 5s, with exactly five 2s and five 5s.
Arrangements: 10!/(5!5!)=252.
solution: The product $100000=2^5\cdot5^5$ has no factor of $3$ or $7$, so the prime digits used can only be $2$ and $5$. To get $2^5\cdot5^5$ we need exactly five $2$s and five $5$s among the ten digits. The number of arrangements is $\dfrac{10!}{5!\,5!}=252.$

hints: A call and put with the same strike/expiry have equal gamma.
Straddle = call + put, so its gamma is double.
solution: By put–call parity the put and call (same strike/expiry) have identical gamma, so a straddle's gamma is twice the call's: $2\times0.03=0.06.$

hints: Put a single white ball in one urn and everything else in the other.
That urn gives survival 1; the other gives 49/99. You pick an urn at 50/50.
solution: Place one white ball alone in urn 1 and the remaining $49$ white $+50$ black in urn 2. Urn 1 guarantees a white; urn 2 gives $\tfrac{49}{99}$. Picking an urn at random, survival is $\tfrac12\cdot1+\tfrac12\cdot\tfrac{49}{99}=\tfrac{74}{99}.$
